Real Analysis

Boundedness and the Algebra of Limits

Boundedness and the Algebra of Limits

Limit of a Sequence

We recall the definition of convergence from the previous lecture, since everything in this lecture builds directly on manipulating this ε\varepsilonε–NNN statement algebraically.

Limit of a Sequence

A sequence (xn)(x_n)(xn​) converges to xxx if ∀ϵ>0, ∃N(ϵ)∈N such that ∀n≥N(ϵ), ∣xn−x∣<ϵ.\forall \epsilon > 0,\ \exists N(\epsilon) \in \mathbb{N} \text{ such that } \forall n \ge N(\epsilon),\ |x_n - x| < \epsilon.∀ϵ>0, ∃N(ϵ)∈N such that ∀n≥N(ϵ), ∣xn​−x∣<ϵ. We write xn→xx_n \to xxn​→x as n→∞n \to \inftyn→∞.

Bounded Sequences

Before combining limits algebraically, it is useful to establish a basic structural fact: convergence forces a sequence to stay within some fixed range. This mirrors the notion of a bounded set from Lecture 3, applied now to the set of terms a sequence produces.

Bounded Sequence

A sequence (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is bounded if there exists M>0M > 0M>0 such that ∣xn∣≤M|x_n| \le M∣xn​∣≤M for all n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N. Equivalently, the set {xn:n∈N}\{x_n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}{xn​:n∈N} is bounded.

Convergent Sequences Are Bounded

The idea of the proof is simple: once nnn is large enough, the tail of the sequence is trapped near the limit xxx and hence automatically bounded; the only remaining concern is the finitely many terms before that point, which can always be bounded by taking a maximum.

Convergent Sequences Are Bounded

Every convergent sequence (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is bounded.

Proof

Let xn→xx_n \to xxn​→x. Choose ϵ=1\epsilon = 1ϵ=1. Then there exists N∈NN \in \mathbb{N}N∈N such that ∣xn−x∣<1|x_n - x| < 1∣xn​−x∣<1 for all n≥Nn \ge Nn≥N. By the triangle inequality (Lecture 3), for all n≥Nn \ge Nn≥N, ∣xn∣≤∣xn−x∣+∣x∣<1+∣x∣.|x_n| \le |x_n - x| + |x| < 1 + |x|.∣xn​∣≤∣xn​−x∣+∣x∣<1+∣x∣. The finitely many terms before index NNN are handled by simply including them in a maximum: M:=max⁡{∣x1∣,∣x2∣,…,∣xN−1∣, 1+∣x∣}.M := \max\big\{|x_1|, |x_2|, \dots, |x_{N-1}|,\ 1 + |x|\big\}.M:=max{∣x1​∣,∣x2​∣,…,∣xN−1​∣, 1+∣x∣}. Then ∣xn∣≤M|x_n| \le M∣xn​∣≤M for all n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N, so (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is bounded.

The converse fails

Boundedness does not imply convergence — the sequence ((−1)n)\bigl((-1)^n\bigr)((−1)n) from Lecture 6 is bounded but does not converge, since it oscillates between two fixed values without settling near either. Boundedness is therefore a necessary, but not sufficient, condition for convergence.

Algebra of Limits

With boundedness established as a basic tool, we now turn to the central computational engine of this lecture: showing that limits interact with the usual arithmetic operations exactly as one would hope.

Limit Laws for Sequences

Suppose xn→xx_n \to xxn​→x and yn→yy_n \to yyn​→y as n→∞n \to \inftyn→∞, and let c∈Rc \in \mathbb{R}c∈R. Then:

  • Addition: xn+yn→x+yx_n + y_n \to x + yxn​+yn​→x+y.
  • Subtraction: xn−yn→x−yx_n - y_n \to x - yxn​−yn​→x−y.
  • Scalar multiplication: c xn→c xc\,x_n \to c\,xcxn​→cx.
  • Product: xnyn→xyx_n y_n \to xyxn​yn​→xy.
  • Reciprocal: if y≠0y \neq 0y=0 and yn≠0y_n \neq 0yn​=0 for all sufficiently large nnn, then 1/yn→1/y1/y_n \to 1/y1/yn​→1/y.
  • Quotient: if y≠0y \neq 0y=0 and yn≠0y_n \neq 0yn​=0 for all sufficiently large nnn, then xn/yn→x/yx_n/y_n \to x/yxn​/yn​→x/y.

The reciprocal and quotient rules both carry a hypothesis — yn≠0y_n \neq 0yn​=0 eventually — that might look like it needs to be assumed separately for each application. In fact it is automatic whenever y≠0y \neq 0y=0, which is worth recording before using these rules.

Note on Reciprocals and Quotients

If y≠0y \neq 0y=0 and yn→yy_n \to yyn​→y, then there exists NNN such that for all n≥Nn \ge Nn≥N, ∣yn∣≥∣y∣/2>0,|y_n| \ge |y|/2 > 0,∣yn​∣≥∣y∣/2>0, so yn≠0y_n \neq 0yn​=0 eventually. This is what justifies the reciprocal and quotient rules — the “eventually nonzero” hypothesis is not an extra assumption but a consequence of yn→y≠0y_n \to y \neq 0yn​→y=0, obtained by taking ϵ=∣y∣/2\epsilon = |y|/2ϵ=∣y∣/2 in the definition of convergence and applying the reverse triangle inequality (Lecture 3) to ∣yn∣≥∣y∣−∣yn−y∣|y_n| \ge |y| - |y_n - y|∣yn​∣≥∣y∣−∣yn​−y∣.

Proof of the Addition Law

The remaining limit laws are all proved by the same basic strategy — split the target error ϵ\epsilonϵ into pieces (typically ϵ/2\epsilon/2ϵ/2 or smaller fractions), control each piece using the convergence of xnx_nxn​ and yny_nyn​ separately, then recombine via the triangle inequality. The addition law is the cleanest illustration of this pattern.

Addition

Let ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0. Since xn→xx_n \to xxn​→x, there exists Nx(ϵ/2)N_x(\epsilon/2)Nx​(ϵ/2) such that for n≥Nx(ϵ/2)n \ge N_x(\epsilon/2)n≥Nx​(ϵ/2), ∣xn−x∣<ϵ/2.|x_n - x| < \epsilon/2.∣xn​−x∣<ϵ/2. Since yn→yy_n \to yyn​→y, there exists Ny(ϵ/2)N_y(\epsilon/2)Ny​(ϵ/2) such that for n≥Ny(ϵ/2)n \ge N_y(\epsilon/2)n≥Ny​(ϵ/2), ∣yn−y∣<ϵ/2.|y_n - y| < \epsilon/2.∣yn​−y∣<ϵ/2. Set N(ϵ)=max⁡{Nx(ϵ/2),Ny(ϵ/2)}N(\epsilon) = \max\{N_x(\epsilon/2), N_y(\epsilon/2)\}N(ϵ)=max{Nx​(ϵ/2),Ny​(ϵ/2)}. Then for all n≥N(ϵ)n \ge N(\epsilon)n≥N(ϵ),

∣(xn+yn)−(x+y)∣=∣(xn−x)+(yn−y)∣≤∣xn−x∣+∣yn−y∣<ϵ/2+ϵ/2=ϵ.\begin{aligned} |(x_n + y_n) - (x+y)| &= |(x_n - x) + (y_n - y)| \\ &\le |x_n - x| + |y_n - y| \\ &< \epsilon/2 + \epsilon/2 = \epsilon. \end{aligned}∣(xn​+yn​)−(x+y)∣​=∣(xn​−x)+(yn​−y)∣≤∣xn​−x∣+∣yn​−y∣<ϵ/2+ϵ/2=ϵ.​

Thus xn+yn→x+yx_n + y_n \to x + yxn​+yn​→x+y.

The template for the remaining laws

Subtraction follows by the identical argument applied to xn+(−yn)x_n + (-y_n)xn​+(−yn​). Scalar multiplication is even simpler: for c≠0c \neq 0c=0, choose NNN so that ∣xn−x∣<ϵ/∣c∣|x_n - x| < \epsilon/|c|∣xn​−x∣<ϵ/∣c∣, giving ∣cxn−cx∣=∣c∣∣xn−x∣<ϵ|cx_n - cx| = |c||x_n-x| < \epsilon∣cxn​−cx∣=∣c∣∣xn​−x∣<ϵ (the case c=0c=0c=0 is trivial). The product and quotient laws are more delicate — they additionally require the boundedness of convergent sequences proved above, since bounding a term like ∣xnyn−xy∣|x_ny_n - xy|∣xn​yn​−xy∣ typically involves inserting and subtracting a cross term such as xnyn−xny+xny−xyx_ny_n - x_ny + x_ny - xyxn​yn​−xn​y+xn​y−xy, then bounding xnx_nxn​ using its boundedness while yn−yy_n - yyn​−y and xn−xx_n - xxn​−x are made small.
